Welcome to session number four and our final session of the Magnetic Email Marketing System and Toolkit. In this session, you discover different types of email campaigns you should be sending, including powerful holiday campaigns. We're going to discuss advanced email strategies and we're going to answer some of those frequently asked questions and of course your questions as well. What different types of things can we be doing in email? Well, so you can just push content out if you wish and there's a justification for some of that but by and large, you're doing one of two things. You want to do one of two things. You want to create at least engagement. So there is some active participation for which there can be a call to action. So that's your go and fetch template to get this, get that. That's Travis's, here's 10 things you can click and you can see the videos about these 10 different products. Just so everybody knows he's referring to stuff that's in the manual. In case a lot of people just came on to watch it. It's, well if they're late, tough. I mean that can be contests, it can be sending this, go here, post that, put the picture of you and your dog eating jiffy peanut butter up on the website and the funniest dog wins a prize. Some sort of engagement and or lead generation or sale. Those two categories are the only things that really let us measure in any way, shape, or form. It's like being in a theater and you can't see past the first row. Is there anybody out there? And so the only way we know there's anybody out there paying attention is if they have to do something as a result of reading it. Absolutely. It's the fallacy by the way in all the online push. So where all we're measuring is views or visits. It's no better or worse than measuring eyeballs watching a TV show. What counts is that it's selling a soap and do we know if it's sold in a soap? That's right. And that's what we're going to be talking about here is how to sell some soap and how to sell lots of soap using email. So let's talk about the different types of special email promotions we should be sending out during the year. So I just want to clarify things for people. So first of all we talked about subject lines, getting the email open. Well first of all we talked about capturing the email address. Then we talked about subject lines that allow people to make people open the email. Then how to write an email that gets actual results. But now let's say we run out of the follow up campaign. Now we're just going to be emailing them on a weekly or daily or whatever basis it is. So what different types of email promotions should we be using? So one is you can use your calendar. So if you're looking for ideas, the calendar gives you pretty much an idea day, an idea week, an idea month. There are the obvious seasonal and holiday situations. But far beyond that there are, because one of the things US Congress does is they designate the official peanut butter day and the official corn growers day. And there are more than one things given the same day by the way. And they do it for constituencies over. So there's this giant list of days that have been assigned to jelly beans and weeks of and months of. There are holidays now that expand. Halloween is now a month. It's not a day. Easter is now two weeks. It's not a weekend. So the calendar gives you really both mainstream opportunities and obscure entertaining opportunities and or opportunities specifically relevant to your particular market. So we all know about Veterans Day and Memorial Day, but I think there's 22 different holidays during the course of the year that have something to do with veterans. So there's the anniversary of the end of World War II. There's the day Vietnam ended. There's all these days. And so if you market to veterans, that calendar is your friend. And so you want to use the calendar if you're looking for ideas. You definitely want to use the news media and current events and all that we talked about if you're looking for ideas. You have big things. Maybe a presidential election in while the campaign really is starting now. Right. 2016 it will be the news lead barring some cataclysmic catastrophic event in the world and it will have legs for the entire year. You can start playing with that fairly early. You can engage people late in the game, movie theaters. You pick a red straw or a blue straw depending on whether you predict that the Republican or the Democrat way in advance last night MSNBC. I forget the names of their shows. They change them often because nobody's watching. They're watching the right. But they did. I actually did a very funny thing. So they had five experts on and they mimicked the NFL draft and had people draft candidates and now each of these five people is stuck with a team of candidates and now they will track this all the way through the election. That's pretty cool. Who's up, who's down, who, right. So you could literally have done the same thing and now have a series of emails about that. So you want to look at your calendar. You want to look at your news media. You want to look at the industry profession, niche subculture that you are in. If you are a motorcycle dealer, you know, there are certain times during the year there are big events that are on motorcycles. So you definitely want to go there and you want to play a lot for some level of response, if not a sale, then a request for a specific piece of information or to see something in action and if not that, some other kind of engagement. Some reason to mail. A reason why you're mailing is basically what you're looking for here. So let's look at some examples of different holiday promotions that we have here and you can just follow along with me. Do we have those holiday promotions up there? All right. So by the way, you get the entire Larry Leprechaun campaign and the reason I say that is because it is a campaign. It was an ongoing story. So this was basically a sale that was happening. So Larry the Leprechaun actually had kidnapped me and it's taken over the whole thing. Same thing as the mouse, the same thing. But it was told in Larry the Leprechaun and it got so much traction that I just kept adding to it. And then Patrick's Day sale last. I think there's four or five emails in the Larry the Leprechaun. And at the time I wrote this, it was the Who Shot JR thing and somebody shot Larry the Leprechaun, which then... You'll see the entire campaign, but the point here is it was fun. It was entertaining and the results were absolutely astronomical. And then as it went on, the open rates got higher and higher and higher because every one was left on a cliffhanger. So they wanted to see what the next segment of Larry the Leprechaun was. Now you don't have to do something like that, but I know people who have seen this before have taken it and modeled it. It works really well. Let's go to the next one, which is much simpler. So you don't have to get sophisticated like that. It can be really, really simple. This is from Nightingale Conant. And you are going to be front and positive because they come with at least one other person and in many cases plus three. Plus if you're any good at all at converting first time customers to repeat customers, the extra couples that are brought in that way who might not ever be brought in any other way. So looping back to the conversation about the every name on the list is worth a dollar. So one way to think about that is what does it cost you to get a name on a list, a lead, or most importantly a customer any other way. So we've got this sequence and it's a sequence. And the event is an event. It's an event, right? So again, don't think that this is just for the online guys. But one of the templates that we included here is the basic sequence that we use at GKIC. And that we use, we beat the hell out of this sequence. Why? Because it works, all right? It includes, and here's basically how this is structured. Let's say we're promoting an event like this. Typically, let's say it's on a Thursday. So we'll send out a Save the Date email on Friday, all right? So the Save the Date email basically gets people excited. It builds anticipation. And it tells them in that email. By the way, the exact template is in the toolkit. But you want to write this down. So this email gets them excited. It builds anticipation for the actual thing. And it tells them on Monday morning at 10 o'clock, we're going to be sending you an email. It's going to have this subject line, all right? And you're going to want to click, when you get it, click and open immediately because you're going to want to register for the things because seating is limited. That's the basic psychology. But it's creating anticipation. It's also planting in their head to look for this email that's coming up. So then on Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, we send out emails promoting the event that we're doing. So if you were part of this, if you're on this, you obviously got some of these emails, all right? On the day of the event, here's what we do. We send out one first thing in the morning. First thing in the morning. We then send out one hour before the thing actually starts. And then, as Marge reminded me, the other one that we send out is right after the thing starts, all right? Saying, hey, you signed up for this thing, you're missing it. You're missing it, all right? And this campaign works like gangbusters. And we have literally given it to you. So if you're doing any type of promotion at all, if you're doing tele-seminars or webinars or live events or you're doing something in the store and you want to make sure people actually show up, this is how you do it. Because without this, you can have a lot of people sign up, but if they don't show up, it doesn't make any difference. And look, if you have to do it by, only by offline means, you can't really get to them at the end. You can't get to them at the end. So you lose that five day or so window. The only way you can do is with phone. And so people will think, well, reminders the same day, by then they're either going to know, and I can tell you in the physical world, getting them to show up at a preview seminar, there is in a physical location, there is productivity from that morning of the day of the meeting. Because again, I think people underestimate everything else that is pulling at somebody and how their initial impulses and intentions disintegrate day to day, even hour to hour, has what they said they were going to do draws near. You can, it's sort of easy to register to be at a four hour webcast or come to a hotel meeting or come to a implant seminar on Tuesday evening at the dentist's office and bring your dad. But as you get closer and closer to the actual act of following through on that, yeah, there's other people grabbing at you. Something's happened. Something's happened. You had a bad day yesterday. There's all this stuff. And if you're not there to counteract it, your drop off of people who are participating is huge. Way back in multi-level, when we did opportunity meetings at hotels, we called it the wish line. That's all the people outside the hotel in the parking lot wish and they pick up their guests because their guests don't show up on their own. And this is really a pick up the guest at the last minute thing. That's really what it is. And the thing to think about and the thing that we're not expressing is we're not showing you the templates because you really can't see them on the screen and they come in the toolkit anyway. Is it's more than just saying, hey, remember you signed up for this thing. I see a lot of people doing that. It's not about reminders. It's not about reminders. It's reselling. Persuading. Motivating. Reselling. Yes. In some cases, shaming. Absolutely. And all of that psychology has worked into those templates, which is really important. There are your example earlier of the word hey in front of the name. If you do, which the applications where you can do it legally are fewer and fewer, but if you do voice broadcast, that leaves a message. The magic words are sorry, I missed you. That's what makes the message that was left seem like a real message. If you don't know to put those words in, the rest of the script, it won't get nearly the result that that will. Let's talk about a very underutilized use of email, which is creating that instant cash flow surge. In the original magnetic marketing program, the reason I bought magnetic marketing, by the way, was for one letter, one thing that you said, which was, hey, there's a cash flow surge letter in there that you can take in, that you can model, and that you can use, that absolutely works. And I needed a cash flow surge in a big, bad kind of way. So talk about with email, my goodness, it's even easier than ever before. Talk about the power of email as far as cash flow surge. So the Nightingale example you showed is essentially that. It's a very promotional price discount deadline driven thing. And again, you don't want to do too much of that. Or you destroy all customer value. It's sort of the problem that the Kohl's and Macy's and Penny's got themselves into. And the Penny CEO who came over from Apple made an ill-fated attempt at too abruptly fixing it by just wiping out coupons and sales. So you get yourself into a position. I mean, nobody is going to go to a Joseph A. Bank store and buy anything at retail. Ever. Ever. They're in the, for the price of one business until they go out of business and there's nothing they can do about it. You don't want to get yourself in that position. But most cash flow surge situations are aggressively promotional. They're generally going to drive by discount and or gift with purchase. The better drive is when you can drive it by limited availability or exclusivity, perhaps also with price. And they're going to be very tightly timed. So urgency matters a lot when you are trying to create a surge. And again, email is perfectly suited to this. See there's a thing that happens. So it's the, even in the newest cars, your last eighth of a tank of gas disappears seemingly at a much faster pace than the first eighth of a tank. So there's a speed up factor when you're almost out that occurs, which is about everything. So QVC and HSN, home shopping TV that does a countdown very similar to what you did here. Which by the way, there's only half an hour left for people to take advantage of this while this is live and get some really cool bonuses which will be going away. So if you tune in and watch a half a day, if you don't normally do it and you watch QVC, they're a little better at it than HSN, but either one you want to watch, you will see that that A, they're very good at the shrinking quantity of availability, the minutes left before the segment ends and we go on to something else. And they show the units being sold literally by the minute. Now one of the things to watch for is how that almost always speeds up at the very end. If you're doing a Q&A, a tele-seminar call of any kind and you're not getting questions, all you got to do is threaten that you're going to end a call. That's right, I do it going once, going twice, going twice. But all of a sudden you got questions. So what you want to do when you want a surge of anything, if you're self on the front of the room, as you and I do from time to time, you need something to create a stampede. And it's almost always going to be a limited supply of a bonus that is available only for the first X number and X needs to be relatively small in ratio to the total size of the room. So what email allows you to do that without email you really did not have a good way to do is to create that tight timing for your promotional offer. So if it is to come into your store, you really can run the whole email. So if the sale is going to be Friday and Saturday, you can run the whole email campaign, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday and Saturday. And you can create this, oh my God, I've only got two days left. Oh my God, I've only got 48 hours left. I'm down to nine hours left. You'll see Levin Furniture has started to do its X percentage off from 9 to 11 in the morning and then the percentage isn't quite as good from 11 to 1. And then the percentage isn't quite as good as it was from 11 to 1 from 1 to 4. Now they do that in their TV ads. They can be emailing all during that day. There's only 45 minutes left before we go from 40% to 35%. If you're on the way and you're not going to make it, call Mike on your cell phone and he'll give you a 15 minute extension so you can get in here and get your check. You can only do that with this kind of media. And it's powerful. It's very powerful. So Dan, before we, well we've actually already done that. So let's actually get to some questions that people have. We've gotten to a number that we've already talked about list segmentation. All right? So and the importance of that. And again with email it's very, very simple. But here's a question that people often ask is, okay well, I'm very concerned and we kind of addressed this earlier but this is specific about pissing people off and getting opt-outs. I'm all worried about my opt-out rate. Address that a little bit. Well, first of all there are essentially two separate things. Because people opt out for a broader range of reasons than being seriously annoyed with you. So you will more often get the opt-outs because they're pissed off. Not from selling or frequency but from some position you take. For GKIC I cause a certain number of departures pretty much every time I write anything or say anything. I mean I contribute to the whole. You contribute to that in a very grand fashion. I can empty a room. What do you say? What is that saying if you're not pissing somebody off by noon? By noon you ain't doing nothing. I mean... I think some people think it's a joke but it's not a joke. No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, it's a quota. It's not a joke at all. See the only place where vanilla really wins is literally in the ice cream business. Every place else it does not win. It is how the Republicans lost the last presidential election and it's how they'll lose this presidential election is there'll be vanilla. And so... But the opt-out thing is of course the bigger problem. But the first thing you have to realize is there's a lot of reasons people opt out that have nothing to do with anything you're doing and if you didn't do it they would still opt out anyway or they're opted out and you don't know it. So if they're not paying attention... Like in our example from Alexis earlier, I sent him an email every single week. But if they're not paying attention to it they're opted out, they just haven't officially opted out. It's just like, you know, there's a lot of people walking around who are dead, they just haven't been legally pronounced dead by the coroner and put in a hole. But they're intellectually, emotionally dead. Same deal here, right? So the opt-out number often reflects people that opted out quite some time ago from a value standpoint and now this week, because you emailed them three times instead of two, it sort of reminded them. That, oh, I really don't want to get this at all. So some of these losses are of no consequence whatsoever and trying to prevent them is of negative consequence. Then another batch of them happen not because of the reasons people think. It's not frequency, it's not commercial, grass commercial messages. It is about being uninteresting, boring, having a bad relationship or delivering grass commercial messages that aren't relevant. See, that's your opt-out problem. It's not the surface stuff that everybody sweats. Oh, if I email them three times a week, that'll, no, it wasn't the three times a week. It's who they are, who they see you as and what you emailed them. Yeah, is it relevant? Is it not relevant? That's, you know, those are the issues. You mentioned this, you mentioned the word earlier and I have it in all my presentations that I do at the boot camp and my segment on email marketing, which is about relationships too. It's about building this relationship and the stronger relationship you have with your list. I mean, you can't abuse the relationship, but the stronger relationship you have with the list, the more opt-out opens you're going to have, the more click-throughs you're going to have, the less opt-outs you're going to have. Well, even the more forgiveness you have for occasionally pissing somebody off. You buy a certain amount of, I'm going to shrug it off. I don't like what he said, but so what versus, oh, we need to go hang them because of what he said. And so relationship equity is built through frequency, consistency, constancy of communication, which email allows you to do more of than you might otherwise be able to afford. But it has got to be the right communication and the right mix of communication. It goes back to the system market match. It's the same thing. So email marketing on the backend as part of the customer relationship is a very powerful tool and GIC uses it effectively in my name and not in my name. And clients use it. I mean, I figured it up before we came over here today of emails pulled out of copy I wrote for clients. There were at least 4 million of them sent last month. So there's a lot of email going out from really a handful, two handfuls of private clients. But everybody has to be very careful to make sure that it's productive. And this is helpful to all. This is what this system is about. It's to make it, first of all, productive, give you some new tools that you don't have and then actually give you the tools to actually take and actually do it so you get results with it. All right. So again, we talked about let's just review what we talked about because this has been a lot and this is something you're going to want to watch again, obviously. So we started off by talking about how to build a lead while talking about the need for a lead generation magnet. We gave some examples of lead generation magnets both online and offline. Then we've talked about opt-in. How do we get collect the names of the addresses? So we talked about online and get an offline, the different ways to do it. Then we talked about, all right, here's how you get your emails open and we went through different types of subject lines. Then in addition to that, we talked about how to get your emails read by putting personality into them and all of that. We talked about follow-up sequences and then holiday campaigns and everything like that. Before we get to any questions, do you have any final words of wisdom? Because we got a whole bunch of questions from folks. Any final words of wisdom about this important topic? So look, the only thing I would say is that you want, with anything else, you want to take a holistic approach, not a isolated piece approach. So there's an ancient joke I love about the labor about the guy who goes to the doctor and determines because he can't make any friends and nobody will hang out with him and so forth. Then it turns out the guy's got really horrible halitosis and really horrible body odor and needs prescription drugs really to solve both of these problems, which ultimately when they're solved, nobody will still hang out with him because he has a shitty person of health. So really, if you just go create great subject lines, but then what's there when they open it doesn't cut the mustard. You really haven't accomplished anything. If you don't use your marketing to segment your list so you can be increasingly relevant segment by segment, then you can do four other things right and it doesn't matter. So the only thing I would say about this is that within the context of email marketing, you want to be holistic and then that fits in the context of a broader whole marketing system. And we've actually even talked about that during this email course. And so too many people sort of run around or hop from one passing dog to the next like a fleet and they run over here. Well, they weren't really helping. They run over here and they work on this. They get all excited about a video sales line. That's like putting one new part in an old bad car. It actually does more harm than good because the other parts can't function with it. So you really got to step back and take a look at your entire approach to lead generation customer acquisition, conversion of one time buyer to committed customer, back end sources of revenue and then fit these things together with them. And again, that's why that's not to say you can't reach in here and take a casual surge sequence and if you need cash, filling the blanks and go do it and get some cash because you can. But the more successful approach overall is to view not component parts but integrated process. That's why this was literally built as a system. I think some people who sell this kind of stuff over use that. This was literally built as a system. With that all the pieces fit together and we really given you, I don't think we've left anything out that people would need to know about using email marketing.
